title: ST558_HW7
text:
Reading in the Data
First, load the libraries needed for importing and working with the dataset.

library(readr)
library(dplyr)

✖ yardstick::spec() masks readr::spec()
✖ recipes::step()   masks stats::step()


Next, read the CSV file into R using the read_csv() function from the readr package.

bike <- read_csv(
  "Data/SeoulBikeData.csv",
  locale = locale(encoding = "Windows-1252"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  clean_names()

# convert few of variables to factors
bike <- bike |>
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(date, format = "%d/%m/%Y"),
    across(
      c(seasons, holiday, functioning_day),
      as.factor
    )
  )

Finally, inspect the data to verify that it was imported correctly.

head(bike)

The dataset is stored in the object bike, which will be used throughout the analysis. The head() function displays the first few rows, glimpse() provides the structure and variable types, and summary() gives descriptive statistics for each variable.


Check for missing values

missing_values <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(bike),
  Missing_Values = colSums(is.na(bike))
)

missing_values

                                       Variable Missing_Values
date                                       date              0
rented_bike_count             rented_bike_count              0
hour                                       hour              0
temperature_c                     temperature_c              0
humidity_percent               humidity_percent              0
wind_speed_m_s                   wind_speed_m_s              0
visibility_10m                   visibility_10m              0
dew_point_temperature_c dew_point_temperature_c              0
solar_radiation_mj_m2     solar_radiation_mj_m2              0
rainfall_mm                         rainfall_mm              0
snowfall_cm                         snowfall_cm              0
seasons                                 seasons              0
holiday                                 holiday              0
functioning_day                 functioning_day              0


The dataset has no missing values, so no data needed to be removed or filled in before the analysis.


Split the training set

set.seed(10)

bike_split <- initial_split(bike, prop = 0.70)

bike_train <- training(bike_split)
bike_test <- testing(bike_split)

bike_train
